statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
In the arithmetic competition, participants need to achieve the highest possible sum from the cards they have. In the team "fst\_ezik", Vadim has $$$n$$$ cards with numbers $$$a\_i$$$, and Kostya has $$$m$$$ cards with numbers $$$b\_i$$$. In each of the $$$q$$$ rounds, they want to win, but this time the rules of the competition are slightly different from the usual ones.

In each round, the participants are given three numbers $$$x\_i$$$, $$$y\_i$$$, and $$$z\_i$$$. The team "fst\_ezik" must choose exactly $$$z\_i$$$ cards from all the cards they have, but Vadim can choose no more than $$$x\_i$$$ cards from his set, and Kostya can choose no more than $$$y\_i$$$ cards from his set. Help them find the highest possible sum for each of the $$$q$$$ rounds.

Input Format
Each test consists of several test cases. The first line contains a single integer $$$t$$$ $$$(1 \\le t \\le 10^4)$$$ — the number of test cases. The descriptions of the test cases follow.

In the first line of each test case, three integers $$$n$$$, $$$m$$$, $$$q$$$ are given $$$(1 \\le n, m \\le 2 \\cdot 10^5, 1 \\le q \\le 10^5)$$$ — the number of cards Vadim has, the number of cards Kostya has, and the number of rounds in the competition.

The second line contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$a\_i$$$ — the numbers on Vadim's cards $$$(1 \\le a\_i \\le 10^9)$$$.

The third line contains $$$m$$$ integers $$$b\_i$$$ — the numbers on Kostya's cards $$$(1 \\le b\_i \\le 10^9)$$$.

The following $$$q$$$ lines describe the rounds with three integers $$$x\_i$$$, $$$y\_i$$$, $$$z\_i$$$ $$$(0 \\le x\_i \\le n, 0 \\le y\_i \\le m, 0 \\le z\_i \\le x\_i + y\_i)$$$ — the limit on the number of cards Vadim can choose, the limit on the number of cards Kostya can choose, and the number of cards they need to select together.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ across all test cases does not exceed $$$2 \\cdot 10^5$$$, the sum of $$$m$$$ across all test cases does not exceed $$$2 \\cdot 10^5$$$, and the sum of $$$q$$$ across all test cases does not exceed $$$10^5$$$.

Output Format
For each test case, output $$$q$$$ numbers — the highest possible sum for the corresponding round.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
4
3 4 5
10 20 30
1 2 3 4
0 0 0
3 4 7
3 4 4
1 4 4
2 2 4
5 5 2
500000000 300000000 100000000 900000000 700000000
800000000 400000000 1000000000 600000000 200000000
1 4 3
5 2 6
4 4 1
100 100 20 20
100 100 20 20
4 4 5
3 3 6
2 363 711
286 121 102
1 1 1
3 1 1
1 2 0
1 3 2
0 1 0
3 3 3
Output
0
70
64
39
57
2700000000
4200000000
420
711
711
0
997
0
1360

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: If we know how many specific cards Vadim should take, and how many should take Kostya (for example when $$$x + y = z$$$). How can we then obtain the maximum possible sum?

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: We need to quickly compute the sum of the $$$x$$$ maximum elements in array $$$a$$$ and the sum of the $$$y$$$ maximum elements in array $$$b$$$. What should we do to be able to compute this for arbitrary $$$x$$$ and $$$y$$$ in $$$O(1)$$$?

hints.title: Hint 3
hints.content: How can we solve this problem if $$$x = n$$$ and $$$y = m$$$ — that is, each person can choose any number of cards, as long as their total is $$$z$$$?

hints.title: Hint 4
hints.content: If $$$x = n$$$ and $$$y = m$$$, then the optimal set of $$$z$$$ cards consists of $$$x'$$$ maximum cards from Vadim and $$$y'$$$ maximum cards from Kostya (where $$$x' + y' = z$$$). How should we adjust this optimal answer if the optimal number for Vadim or Kostya exceeds their limits (that is, if $$$x \lt x'$$$ or $$$y \lt y'$$$)?

solutions.title: Editorial
solutions.content:
2132E - Arithmetics Competition
Solution 1
If for a given query $$$(x, y, z)$$$ we fix $$$x'$$$ and $$$y'$$$ ($$$x' + y' = z$$$)  — the number of cards taken by Vadim and Kostya respectively, then the maximum sum can be obtained by taking the maximum $$$x'$$$ elements from array $$$a$$$ and the maximum $$$y'$$$ elements from array $$$b$$$.
For convenience, let's sort the arrays of cards in descending order. Now, to quickly compute the sum of the $$$k$$$ maximum elements in some array, we need to calculate the prefix sums of the sorted arrays $$$a$$$ and $$$b$$$.
Now we will find the optimal answer for each value of $$$z$$$ under the condition that there are no restrictions on $$$x$$$ and $$$y$$$. To do this, we need to "merge" two sorted arrays. We will use two pointers to traverse them, keeping track of how many elements have been taken from the first array and how many from the second. Then we will be able to find the optimal values $$$x'(z)$$$ and $$$y'(z)$$$  — how many maximum cards Vadim and Kostya should take for a given value of $$$z$$$, if they have no other restrictions.
Now we just need to understand how to find the answer if there are restrictions on $$$x$$$ and $$$y$$$, and the optimal answers do not fit within these restrictions. Let $$$x'  \gt  x$$$, where $$$x'$$$ is the optimal answer for the given $$$z$$$. Then it is sufficient to take exactly $$$x$$$ maximum cards from array $$$a$$$, and take the remaining $$$z - x$$$ maximum cards from array $$$b$$$.
This will be the optimal answer because all the cards taken from array $$$a$$$ will be at least as large as all the cards that have not yet been taken from array $$$b$$$, meaning there is no point in making additional swaps of cards from one array to another.
In total, we will need $$$O(n \log n + m \log m)$$$ for sorting the arrays, $$$O(n + m)$$$ for finding the prefix sums and optimal values $$$x'(z)$$$ and $$$y'(z)$$$, as well as $$$O(1)$$$ for each query. In total, the complexity of this solution is $$$O(n \log n + m \log m + q)$$$.
Solution 2
This problem can also be solved using ternary search. If we iterate over the value of $$$x'$$$ in the interval $$$[max(0, z - y), min(x, z)]$$$, we can find the maximum value of the function $$$f(x') = a\_pref[x'] + b\_pref[z - x']$$$. Note that this function is convex, as increasing $$$x'$$$ means we are adding smaller cards from $$$a$$$ and removing larger cards from $$$b$$$. (More formally, $$$\Delta f(t) = f(t) - f(t - 1) = a[t] - b[z - t + 1]$$$ and $$$\Delta f(t) \ge \Delta f(t + 1)$$$). This means we can use ternary search to find the global maximum of this function.
In total, we will need $$$O(n \log n + m \log m)$$$ for sorting the arrays, $$$O(n + m)$$$ for finding the prefix sums, and $$$O(\log(n + m))$$$ in the worst case for each query. In total, the complexity of this solution is $$$O(n \log n + m \log m + q \log(n + m))$$$.
